output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.5": 0.95, "R1.6": 0.8, "R1.7": 0.5}}

Summary: I flag concrete problems (one paragraph is off-topic boilerplate, ordering buries the contribution, and a key claim lacks supporting evidence) and provide a reorganized revision.

## Substantive issues

1. **The "PDE convention / Adam / MLP depth 5" paragraph is misplaced and partly inert.** This is methods-detail for individual training runs, not part of an introduction/overview. Worse, two of its sentences ("analytical solutions … do not require re-derivation," "matching common practice") carry no information that affects the contribution. Move the operational details to Section 3 (or a methods section) and delete the throat-clearing.

2. **The contribution is buried.** The dataset (PINN-FAIL), the taxonomy, and the predictive classifier are three distinct contributions, but the current ordering interleaves them with annotation procedure, a finding (41% imbalance), a polemical claim (misdiagnosis has slowed progress), and unrelated training hyperparameters. A reader can't tell on first pass whether the paper's primary contribution is the dataset, the taxonomy, or the early-warning classifier. State all three up front, then expand.

3. **The "misdiagnosis has slowed methodological progress" claim is unsupported in this excerpt.** You assert that proposed fixes target boundary weighting when the pathology is spectral, but you don't cite which fixes, nor show evidence that reclassifying runs under your taxonomy changes the conclusion. Either (a) cite 2–3 representative "boundary-weighting fix" papers and quantify how many of your spectral-bias-collapse runs they would have mis-targeted, or (b) soften to a hypothesis.

4. **Annotator-agreement reporting is incomplete.** Cohen's kappa of 0.74 is reported, but: across how many categories? Two annotators on all 1,200 runs, or a sample? What was the disagreement rate per failure family — uniform, or concentrated in the boundary-vs-spectral confusion you later highlight? The latter would directly substantiate point 3.

5. **The classifier result needs minimal framing to be credible.** "78% accuracy by iteration 5,000" — out of how many classes (presumably 4, so chance is 25%)? Train/test split? Held-out PDEs or held-out runs? Without this, the number is unanchored.

6. **Novelty positioning is thin.** The analogy to "profiling datasets … for systems benchmarks" is evocative but vague. Name one or two such datasets (MLPerf traces, DeepBench, etc.) and state what PINN-FAIL borrows from them and what it does differently. Also: prior PINN failure-mode work (Krishnapriyan et al. 2021 on failure modes; Wang et al. on NTK/spectral bias; causality-respecting PINNs from Wang & Perdikaris) should be acknowledged so the taxonomy's delta is visible.

7. **Internal forward-reference is awkward.** "Section 3 details our annotation protocol" sitting as a standalone paragraph reads like a stray note. Fold it into the contributions paragraph or remove.

8. **Limitations paragraph is fine but could be tightened and moved.** In an introduction, a one-sentence scope statement suffices; the full limitations discussion belongs at the end of the paper.

## Suggested revision

> Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) often fail to converge on PDE problems that classical solvers handle routinely, yet the field lacks a principled taxonomy of these failures. Existing diagnoses tend to be case-by-case: a run is labeled "boundary-imbalanced" or "spectrally biased" based on the author's prior, with little cross-paper agreement on what those labels mean operationally.
>
> We make three contributions. **(i)** We release **PINN-FAIL**, a labeled dataset of 1,200 PINN training runs spanning 8 benchmark PDEs, each annotated with a dominant failure signature. **(ii)** We propose a four-family taxonomy — residual–boundary imbalance, spectral bias collapse, causality violation in time-dependent PDEs, and ill-conditioned Jacobians near sharp gradients — grounded in the dataset rather than in isolated case studies. **(iii)** We show the taxonomy is *predictive*: a lightweight classifier trained on early-iteration loss trajectories identifies the eventual failure mode with 78% accuracy (4-way; chance 25%) by iteration 5,000, enabling early abort or mode-specific remediation.
>
> Two findings drive our central methodological claim. First, residual–boundary imbalance accounts for 41% of observed failures, making it the dominant mode. Second, spectral bias collapse is systematically confused with boundary imbalance because the two produce nearly indistinguishable early-loss curves; in our annotation, [X]% of inter-annotator disagreements concentrated on this pair. We argue — and quantify in Section 5 — that this confusion has misdirected a portion of recent remediation work toward boundary reweighting when the underlying pathology is spectral [cite 2–3 representative works].
>
> PINN-FAIL extends the empirical-characterization tradition that profiling datasets [cite] established for systems benchmarks, and complements prior PINN failure-mode studies [Krishnapriyan et al. 2021; Wang et al.] by providing a shared, labeled substrate rather than isolated diagnostic studies. Our coverage is currently biased toward second-order parabolic and hyperbolic equations and excludes operator-learning architectures (DeepONet, FNO); we discuss both in Section 7.

(The annotation rubric, kappa breakdown by family, optimizer/architecture choices, and L2-error protocol move to Section 3.)

## Uncertainty disclosure

- I don't know the actual concentration of inter-annotator disagreement by failure family — the "[X]%" placeholder must be filled from your data, or the sentence cut.
- The prior-work citations I named (Krishnapriyan 2021, Wang/Perdikaris on causality and NTK) are standard PINN-failure references but I have not verified the exact framing each uses against your taxonomy; confirm before citing.
- The claim that some remediation papers mis-target boundary weighting is plausible but you must produce the receipts; I'm recommending the rhetorical move, not vouching for the empirical claim.
